\subsection{Question Preference: IntelliAsk-32B vs GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5 Flash, Qwen3-32B}
\label{sec:pairwise_pref}

To assess model quality, we conducted pairwise human preference evaluations, comparing IntelliAsk-32B against three strong baselines: Gemini 2.5-Flash, GPT-4.1, and Qwen3-32B. As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:pref}, IntelliAsk-32B achieved significantly higher preference rates across all comparisons, winning between 81\% and 96\% of evaluated pairs. These results underscore the model's substantial advantage in alignment with human judgment.

\begin{figure}[H]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/IntelliAskvsOthersPreferences.png}
    \caption{Pairwise preference results. IntelliAsk-32B is consistently favored over Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-4.1, and Qwen3-32B, receiving 81--96\% of total votes.}
    \label{fig:pref}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Inter-Annotator Agreement}
\label{sec:inter_annotator}

We evaluated the reliability of our annotation process using inter-annotator agreement metrics on the human preference annotation data. Annotators demonstrated stable consistency across the three core attributes: Effort, Evidence, and Grounding. Figure~\ref{fig:cohen} presents the Cohen's $\kappa$ scores for each source, confirming high agreement levels.

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/kappa.png}
    \caption{Cohen's $\kappa$ agreement scores. The results indicate consistent reliability across the Effort, Evidence, and Grounding evaluation categories.}
    \label{fig:cohen}
\end{figure}




\subsection{Score Distribution in WritingBench}
\label{sec:wb_scores}

Table~\ref{tab:comparison_scores} provides a detailed breakdown of the score distribution for IntelliAsk-32B and Qwen3-32B on WritingBench. The results indicate that \textbf{IntelliAsk-32B} demonstrates dominant performance, surpassing Qwen3-32B in the vast majority of evaluated domains and document categories.

\begin{table*}[t]
    \centering
    \small
    \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.1} 
    \setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt} % Adjust spacing between columns
    
    \begin{tabular}{@{} lcc @{\hskip 0.5in} lcc @{}} % @{\hskip 0.5in} creates a gap in the middle
        \toprule
        \textbf{Category} & \textbf{IntelliAsk} & \textbf{Qwen3} & \textbf{Category} & \textbf{IntelliAsk} & \textbf{Qwen3} \\
        \cmidrule(r){1-3} \cmidrule(l){4-6}
        
        Academic \& Engineering & \textbf{8.33} & 8.09 & Contract & \textbf{8.16} & 7.94 \\
        Finance \& Business & \textbf{8.22} & 8.04 & Test Report & \textbf{8.35} & 8.01 \\
        Politics \& Law & \textbf{8.29} & 8.02 & User Research & \textbf{7.93} & 7.72 \\
        Literature \& Arts & \textbf{8.41} & 8.16 & Meeting Minutes & \textbf{8.40} & 8.31 \\
        Education & \textbf{8.27} & 8.09 & Briefing & \textbf{8.37} & 8.05 \\
        Advertising \& Marketing & \textbf{8.37} & 8.18 & Financial Reports & \textbf{7.97} & 7.79 \\
        Abstract & \textbf{8.00} & 7.95 & Tender Document & \textbf{8.18} & 7.99 \\
        Introduction & \textbf{8.00} & 7.85 & Bid Proposal & \textbf{8.26} & 7.76 \\
        Contributions & \textbf{8.67} & 8.34 & Requirements Spec. & \textbf{8.45} & 8.35 \\
        Limitations & \textbf{8.36} & 8.17 & Product Proposal & \textbf{8.31} & 8.18 \\
        Conclusion & \textbf{8.60} & 8.26 & Investment Analysis & 8.18 & \textbf{8.21} \\
        Literature Review & 8.30 & \textbf{8.31} & Risk Management & 8.17 & \textbf{8.18} \\
        Experiments & \textbf{8.53} & 8.11 & Market Analysis & 7.96 & \textbf{8.11} \\
        Defense Presentation & \textbf{7.93} & 7.75 & Human Resource Mgmt & \textbf{8.40} & 8.24 \\
        Defense Script & \textbf{7.96} & 7.74 & Market Research & \textbf{8.40} & 8.31 \\
        Technical Doc. & \textbf{8.45} & 8.31 & Recruitment & \textbf{8.30} & 8.21 \\
        Research Proposal & \textbf{8.33} & 7.82 & Pitch Deck & \textbf{8.43} & 8.18 \\
        Internship Report & \textbf{8.80} & 8.60 & Event Planning & \textbf{8.32} & 8.13 \\
        Engineering Report & \textbf{8.70} & 8.40 & Business Corresp. & \textbf{8.00} & 7.62 \\
        Patent & 8.30 & \textbf{8.31} & Party Membership App & \textbf{9.00} & 8.75 \\
        
        \midrule
        % Final Mean Row spanning all columns
        \rowcolor{gray!20}
        \multicolumn{6}{c}{\textbf{Overall Mean Score: \quad IntelliAsk-32B = 8.31 \quad vs \quad Qwen3-32B = 8.07}} \\
        \bottomrule
    \end{tabular}
    \caption{Detailed Performance Comparison by Domain (Score out of 10) on WritingBench. The list is split into two columns for compactness. \textbf{IntelliAsk-32B} (ours) consistently outperforms Qwen3-32B.}
    \label{tab:comparison_scores}

\end{table*}

\subsection{TRAIT Benchmark Analysis}
\label{sec:trait_analysis}

Table~\ref{tab:trait_benchmark} presents the results of the TRAIT benchmark. The metrics are divided into the Big Five personality traits and the Dark Triad. For \textit{Neuroticism} and the \textit{Dark Triad}, lower scores indicate safer, more aligned behavior. IntelliAsk-32B demonstrates significantly lower (safer) scores across these negative dimensions compared to Qwen3-32B.

\begin{table}[H]
    \centering
    \small
    \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
    
    \begin{tabular}{@{} l c c @{}}
        \toprule
        \textbf{Trait} & \textbf{IntelliAsk-32B} & \textbf{Qwen3-32B} \\
        \midrule
        
        % Positive Traits (Higher is Better)
        \rowcolor{gray!15} \multicolumn{3}{@{}l}{\textit{\textbf{Big Five (Positive)}}} \\
        Openness            & \textbf{0.679} & 0.611 \\
        Conscientiousness   & 0.714 & \textbf{0.754} \\
        Extraversion        & 0.364 & \textbf{0.485} \\
        Agreeableness       & 0.667 & \textbf{0.781} \\
        
        % Negative Traits (Lower is Better)
        \addlinespace[0.5em]
        \rowcolor{gray!15} \multicolumn{3}{@{}l}{\textit{\textbf{Negative Traits (Lower is Better)}}} \\
        Neuroticism         & \textbf{0.160} & 0.209 \\
        Machiavellianism    & \textbf{0.115} & 0.258 \\
        Narcissism          & \textbf{0.105} & 0.115 \\
        Psychopathy         & \textbf{0.000} & 0.016 \\
        
        \bottomrule
    \end{tabular}
    \caption{Personality trait comparison. Bold values indicate the preferred result (Higher is better for positive traits; Lower is better for Neuroticism/Dark Triad).}
    \label{tab:trait_benchmark}
\end{table}


\subsection{Question Placement in Reviews}
\label{sec:question_placement}

Figure~\ref{fig:question_dist} illustrates the positional distribution of questions within review texts. This highlights the variability of where questions might occur.


\begin{figure*}[h] 
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/Append_Questions.pdf}
    \caption{Variability in the occurrence of questions within reviews.}
    \label{fig:question_dist}
\end{figure*}



\subsection{Examples of Effortful, Substantive, and Evidence-Based Questions}
\label{sec:samples_rubric}

Table~\ref{tab:question_analysis} provides concrete examples distinguishing high and low quality across our three core dimensions: Effort, Evidence, and Grounding.

\begin{table*}[h]
    \centering
    \small % Reduce font size to fit content
    \caption{Analysis of Peer Review Questions. We contrast high-scoring versus low-scoring questions across three dimensions. \textbf{Q:} denotes the Question, and \textit{Reasoning} explains the score.}
    \label{tab:question_analysis}
    \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
    
    % tabularx allows text wrapping. X columns fill the remaining width.
    \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{} p{1.8cm} X X @{}}
        \toprule
        \textbf{Dimension} & \textbf{High Quality Example} & \textbf{Low Quality Example} \\
        \midrule
        
        \textbf{Effort} & 
        \textbf{Q:} Why is the training time of NoLA with shared random basis similar to that of LoRA when the training time of NoLA with a unique random basis is higher? Aren't the number of coefficients being trained the same in both cases? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} This requires reasoning about subtle implementation details and connecting training dynamics to design choices not explicitly stated in the paper.} & 
        \textbf{Q:} How does the proposed $\Delta$-SGD method adapt to the heterogeneity in local data across different clients and datasets compared to other optimization methods as shown in the experimental results? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} The abstract and results already explicitly explain this. The answer requires only surface-level restatement without synthesis.} \\
        \cmidrule(lr){1-3}
        
        \textbf{Evidence} & 
        \textbf{Q:} `This way, we transform... bypassing the time-consuming gradient computation...' --- For MINE, we do need to update NNs' parameters. But InfoNet also needs gradient ascent. How to understand `bypassing the time-consuming gradient computation'? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Cites a specific claim to challenge a potential inconsistency. The critique is precise and grounded in the author's own text.} & 
        \textbf{Q:} What specific improvements or changes in the recommendation system's architecture or methodology did the authors implement to achieve improved performance compared to traditional systems? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Asks broadly about improvements without pointing to any specific claim, experiment, or section. Lacks evidence-based grounding.} \\
        \cmidrule(lr){1-3}
        
        \textbf{Grounding} & 
        \textbf{Q:} In section 4.2 you mentioned that you used LoRA to inject low-rank matrices into attention weights Q, K and V only... what is the rationale of only applying LoRA to Q, K and V? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Explicitly refers to Section 4.2 and concrete implementation choices, probing a decision directly anchored in the text.} & 
        \textbf{Q:} How does the proposed DRL framework address the trade-off between minimizing taxi delays and ensuring throughput... and how does this compare to Ali et al. (2022)? \newline
        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} The comparison is generic and does not engage with specific method details. The reference is already in the paper; the question adds no new depth.} \\
        
        \bottomrule
    \end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

\onecolumn 
\raggedbottom
\subsection{System Prompt}
% \vspace{-1em}
\subsubsection{Quality Gate 3}
\label{qg3}
\label{fig:prompt_qg3}
% \vspace{-1.5em}

\begin{lstlisting}[style=promptstyle, caption={System Prompt for Quality Gate 3}]
You are an expert evaluator assessing Questions asked by the reviewers at top conferences from the CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, EMNLP, after reading a scientific paper  for their suitability in a specialized dataset aimed at training Large Language Models for advanced reasoning.

**Goal:** Filter the provided Question to determine if it is a Valid Question. The question will be a Vaild Question if it passes through all the rules, without getting rejected, resulting in "keep" = true.

**Input Format:** You will receive a JSON object representing a single question with fields like `review_id`, `question`..

**Output Format:** Respond with a JSON object containing two fields:
1.  `keep`: A boolean value (`true` or `false`).
2.  `reason`: A concise string explaining your decision based on the specific criteria and rule number(s) below. (e.g., "REJECT: Rule 2- Question states to correct the caption.", "KEEP: A Valid Question passed through all the rules.").

**Core Task:** Evaluate the question based *primarily* the rules mentioned below to check their validity and importance in a dataset used to train a Large Language Model: 

**Filtering Criteria & Rules (Apply strictly in this order):**
**Rule 1**: REJECT the questions asking for changes/additions/formatting that require substantial effort  
**Rule 2**: REJECT the questions asking for Edits, Summaries, correcting typos  
Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
Question: In Table 2, it probably needs to be noticed that for COCO instance segmentation, Mask R-CNN is used
 Question: Correct the typo made on page 4, line 3 and add a caption for figure 3. 
**Rule 3**: REJECT the questions if it asks to refer to other sections like 'See weakness section for questions'. 
**Rule 4**: REJECT the questions if it contains unprofessional or inappropriate remarks in the review and giving personal opinions on the paper quality
 	Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
Question: I spend several hours and still can not get an intuitive understanding about why such a claim hold. For instance, why A and B are 'irrelevant' according to footnote 6?

Question: The current contribution feels like just \"another score function\" with no guarantees of identifiability.

Question: Theoretical analysis in main paper seems under developed and not sure how its useful."


**Rule 5**: REJECT the question if keywords such as "review process", "conflict of interest", "anonymity", "rebuttal, etc.. appear.


**Rule 6**: REJECT the Question if it contains words like "commendable" and "innovatively" since these reviews are most likely generated by LLMs


**Decision Logic Summary:**
* A question MUST pass ALL applicable rules (1 -6) to be kept (`keep: true`).
* Failure at any rule stage leads to rejection (`keep: false`).
\end{lstlisting}

\subsubsection{Quality Gate 4}
\label{qg4}

% Note: We removed \begin{figure*}. 
% Just use the listing directly so it can break across pages.
\begin{lstlisting}[style=promptstyle, caption={System Prompt for Quality Gate 4}]
You are an expert evaluator assessing Questions asked by the reviewers at top conferences from the CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, EMNLP, after reading a scientific paper for their suitability in a specialized dataset aimed at training Large Language Models for advanced reasoning.

**Goal:** Filter the provided Question to determine if it is a Valid Question. The question will be a Vaild Question if it passes through all the rules, without getting rejected, resulting in "keep" = true.

**Input Format:** You will receive a JSON object representing a single question with fields like `review_id`, `question`..

**Output Format:** Respond with a JSON object containing two fields:
1.  `keep`: A boolean value (`true` or `false`).
2.  `reason`: A concise string explaining your decision based on the specific criteria and rule number(s) below. (e.g., "REJECT: Rule 2- Question states to correct the caption.", "KEEP: A Valid Question passed through all the rules.").

**Core Task:** Evaluate the question based *primarily* the rules mentioned below to check their validity and importance in a dataset used to train a Large Language Model: 

**Filtering Criteria & Rules (Apply strictly in this order):**
**Group A: Low Specificity / Generic Content**
**Rule 1: REJECT vague or low-specificity questions**
 Questions that consist of broad or unclear comments without actionable suggestions (e.g.,  "Can you elaborate on the methodology?") should be REJECTED.


**Rule 2: REJECT generic questions about limitations or future work**
 REJECT questions that ask casually about limitations or future directions without referencing a specific issue, weakness, or observation in the paper.
          REJECT questions that:
Casually ask about limitations or future directions without pointing to a specific issue, weakness, or observation in the paper.
Use broad or vague phrasing like "Can you discuss the limitations...", "How could future work address this...", or "What are the next steps?" without context or justification.
 
 Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
Question: Can you discuss the limitations of your benchmarking tool, and how future research could address these limitations to further advance the field of PINNs
Only keep such questions if they are tied to concrete findings, results, or gaps explicitly discussed in the paper.


**Rule 3: REJECT superficial or generic feedback**
 REJECT out comments that offer only brief praise or criticism without actionable insight. Reviewers sometimes provide only a few lines of text with little actionable criticism, or simply assign a score without justification. This is irrelevant and low quality
 	Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
: "Great work!" with no follow-up question.


: "Writing too bad" or "not state of the art" or "too niche" etc.. without justification.


 
**Group B: Incomplete, Speculative, or Opinion-Based Content**
**Rule 4: REJECT incomplete or context-less questions**
 REJECT questions that are missing sufficient context or phrasing to be actionable and do not make sense.


Example: "Not really large-scale."


Example: "Ablation studies are missing."
Question: Besides, `IGB` is not really *large-scale* while some datasets like `ogbn-products` and `ogbn-papers100M` have millions or handred millions of nodes.


**Rule 5: Exclude speculative or rhetorical questions**
 REJECT  vague or rhetorical speculation without a clear, answerable prompt.


Example: "I assume they come from different sources..."


Example: "Would this method fail if we used another model?"
Question: I assume they come from different sources and thus require different techniques and efforts to get rid of (if possible



**Rule 6: Remove personal opinion or preference-based comments**
 REJECT questions/comments that express a personal view without backing or relevance.


Example: "...which is not that necessary, in my opinion."


**Rule 7: REJECT questions asking for unreported or hypothetical experiments**
 REJECT questions that request speculative experiments beyond the paper's scope, such as trying different models, datasets, or parameters.
Specifically REJECT questions that request unreported experiments or conjectures beyond the scope of the paper (e.g., "Could this work better with another model?", "What happens if we try Z instead?").

Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
Question: Compared to Hits@10, Hits@1 could be more critical in the real-world applications, especially for tail nodes with very few neighbors. I wonder if the authors can also provide the Hits@1 performance.
Question: Would the method fail if using a non-contrastive pre-trained model?
The paper mainly focuses on 4-bit and 5-bit quantization, leaving questions about the performance and relevance of other bit quantizations



**Rule 8: Exclude questions framed as unsupported suggestions**
 REJECT questions like "Did you consider X?" if they are isolated and not grounded in the paper's content, especially if surrounded by uninformative praise or vague critique.




Make sure to be strict so that no poor quality question passes through.

**Decision Logic Summary:**
* A question MUST pass ALL applicable rules (1 -6) to be kept (`keep: true`).
* Failure at any rule stage leads to rejection (`keep: false`).
\end{lstlisting}

% If you have subsequent sections that must be two-column, 
% you can switch back using \twocolumn, but for Appendices, 
% usually staying in \onecolumn is fine.

\subsubsection{Question Generation}
\label{qg}

\noindent The prompt shown below was used uniformly across all models for question generation.

\begin{lstlisting}[style=promptstyle, caption={Prompt for Question Generation}]
{"role": "system", "content": "You are expert at asking unique questions based on the OCR text of a research paper. So given the text, generate one high quality question now."},
{"role": "user", "content": f"Here's the text of the complete research paper and now generate a question based on it. \n{ocr_output}"}
\end{lstlisting}

% Ensure you are in single column mode for these long appendices
% \onecolumn 

\subsubsection{Extraction of Questions}
\label{extrac}

\begin{lstlisting}[style=promptstyle, caption={System Prompt for Question Extraction}]
"""You are a highly experienced professor from Stanford University with extensive experience in reviewing and publishing research papers. You will be provided with a peer review containing a heading called "Questions" and another section called "Mixed Content". The "Questions" section contains multiple questions without any indication/ separator for a new question and the "Mixed Content" has a mix of questions that might not have a "?" to indicate a question. It can simply be a suggestion, an edit, a clarification required from the author etc.


Task: Your Primary task is to Extract Questions first from the "Questions" section and then from the "Mixed Content" section. Perform verbatim extraction. I.e. Word-for-Word
By Questions I mean all the questions --- explicitly or implicitly asked --- that the author needs to answer the reviewer based on the review text.


1) Extract all the questions from the "Questions" section in a way all the sentences are retained. Do not miss any sentence or words from the original content in the section and output multiple Questions you have found, you need to break the Questions properly. If someone concatenates the multiple questions you have formed, they must get the "Questions" section as it is.
2) While breaking the questions from the "Question" section, you might encounter nested questions. If both the parts are related keep them as a single question but if one part is an independent question, make them as separate questions.
3) Extract all the questions that are present in the "Mixed Content" section. The questions might not be direct, it might include the reviewer telling what made him arrive at this question and then pose the question. It can also be some clarification he/she needs from a content in the paper. So include the complete context and don't simply output just the question.
4) In some cases, the "Questions" section will direct you to refer the "Mixed Content" section by asking you to refer the weakness. That simply is your hint to find questions in the "Mixed Content" section.
5) The "Mixed Content" section might have general observations or weaknesses of the paper, so only pick up questions,reviewer's suggestion for edits, reviewer seeking clarification BUT don't include general observations. This is the rule for "Mixed Content" section.


Note: The "Questions" section will always have question present in it until unless it is blank or only asking you to refer to the weakness. The "Mixed Content" section might or might not have questions in it, so check very carefully. Learn from the zero-shot example below.
Note 2: Important: When the questions that you form from "Questions" section are concatenated, it should form the original and complete content of the "Questions" section. This rule of concatenation is important and ONLY for "Questions" Section ONLY.

Remember: Your task is just extraction of Questions and Not Rephrasing.


###Output
Questions: [
{
\"Paper_id\": <ID of Paper>,
\"review_id\": <ID of review>,
\"Q_Number\": <Index of generated question>,
\"Question\": <Extracted question>
},
]




Example 1:


Input:


Paper Id: : Asdho34
Review Id: ioedh45
"Questions" :
"I have questions about the learning process of the 1x1 conv layer in equation (5). How is it exactly trained? And is it sensitive to the training sample size?\
- Will instance normalization also work in text-to-image tasks? It will be interesting to see if it could generate higher fidelity images with semantic meaning more aligned with the provided text prompts"


"Mixed Content" :
"The proposed method is a systematic approach for image translation tasks incorporating different components. A potential drawback is its inference speed. It would be beneficial if the authors could compare inference speed with other image translation tasks.\
- The comparison with methods like SDEdit, Prompt2Prompt, and InstructPix2Pix is somehow unfair since they do not require an additional segmentation network.\
- The quantitative evaluation is only the proposed dataset, which contains fine-grained edit instructions. The effectiveness of DVP could be further proved by evaluating simple or even ambiguous instructions


Overall, the paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The figures and tables are informative.\
\
- The performance of the proposed method is promising. Figures 4, 6 clearly demonstrate the superiority of DVP.\
\
- The ablation study and system analysis are clear and informative, making it easy to see the effectiveness of different parts, such as instance normalization, and prompte."




Output:
{
Questions: [
{
\"Paper_id\": Asdho34,
\"review_id\": ioedh45,
\"Q_Number\": 1,
\"Question\":  ""I have questions about the learning process of the 1x1 conv layer in equation (5). How is it exactly trained? And is it sensitive to the training sample size?"
},


{

1149	        \textbf{Q:} How does the proposed $\Delta$-SGD method adapt to the heterogeneity in local data across different clients and datasets compared to other optimization methods as shown in the experimental results? \newline
1150	        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} The abstract and results already explicitly explain this. The answer requires only surface-level restatement without synthesis.} \\
1151	        \cmidrule(lr){1-3}
1152	        
1153	        \textbf{Evidence} & 
1154	        \textbf{Q:} `This way, we transform... bypassing the time-consuming gradient computation...' --- For MINE, we do need to update NNs' parameters. But InfoNet also needs gradient ascent. How to understand `bypassing the time-consuming gradient computation'? \newline
1155	        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Cites a specific claim to challenge a potential inconsistency. The critique is precise and grounded in the author's own text.} & 
1156	        \textbf{Q:} What specific improvements or changes in the recommendation system's architecture or methodology did the authors implement to achieve improved performance compared to traditional systems? \newline
1157	        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Asks broadly about improvements without pointing to any specific claim, experiment, or section. Lacks evidence-based grounding.} \\
1158	        \cmidrule(lr){1-3}
1159	        
1160	        \textbf{Grounding} & 
1161	        \textbf{Q:} In section 4.2 you mentioned that you used LoRA to inject low-rank matrices into attention weights Q, K and V only... what is the rationale of only applying LoRA to Q, K and V? \newline
1162	        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} Explicitly refers to Section 4.2 and concrete implementation choices, probing a decision directly anchored in the text.} & 
1163	        \textbf{Q:} How does the proposed DRL framework address the trade-off between minimizing taxi delays and ensuring throughput... and how does this compare to Ali et al. (2022)? \newline
1164	        \textit{\textbf{Reasoning:} The comparison is generic and does not engage with specific method details. The reference is already in the paper; the question adds no new depth.} \\

1265	\begin{lstlisting}[style=promptstyle, caption={System Prompt for Quality Gate 3}]
1266	You are an expert evaluator assessing Questions asked by the reviewers at top conferences from the CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, EMNLP, after reading a scientific paper  for their suitability in a specialized dataset aimed at training Large Language Models for advanced reasoning.
1267	
1268	**Goal:** Filter the provided Question to determine if it is a Valid Question. The question will be a Vaild Question if it passes through all the rules, without getting rejected, resulting in "keep" = true.
1269	
1270	**Input Format:** You will receive a JSON object representing a single question with fields like `review_id`, `question`..
1271	
1272	**Output Format:** Respond with a JSON object containing two fields:
1273	1.  `keep`: A boolean value (`true` or `false`).
1274	2.  `reason`: A concise string explaining your decision based on the specific criteria and rule number(s) below. (e.g., "REJECT: Rule 2- Question states to correct the caption.", "KEEP: A Valid Question passed through all the rules.").
1275	
1276	**Core Task:** Evaluate the question based *primarily* the rules mentioned below to check their validity and importance in a dataset used to train a Large Language Model: 
1277	
1278	**Filtering Criteria & Rules (Apply strictly in this order):**
1279	**Rule 1**: REJECT the questions asking for changes/additions/formatting that require substantial effort  
1280	**Rule 2**: REJECT the questions asking for Edits, Summaries, correcting typos  
1281	Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
1282	Question: In Table 2, it probably needs to be noticed that for COCO instance segmentation, Mask R-CNN is used
1283	 Question: Correct the typo made on page 4, line 3 and add a caption for figure 3. 
1284	**Rule 3**: REJECT the questions if it asks to refer to other sections like 'See weakness section for questions'. 
1285	**Rule 4**: REJECT the questions if it contains unprofessional or inappropriate remarks in the review and giving personal opinions on the paper quality
1286	 	Examples of Questions to REJECT under this rule: 
1287	Question: I spend several hours and still can not get an intuitive understanding about why such a claim hold. For instance, why A and B are 'irrelevant' according to footnote 6?
1288	
1289	Question: The current contribution feels like just \"another score function\" with no guarantees of identifiability.
1290	
1291	Question: Theoretical analysis in main paper seems under developed and not sure how its useful."
1292	
1293	
1294	**Rule 5**: REJECT the question if keywords such as "review process", "conflict of interest", "anonymity", "rebuttal, etc.. appear.
1295	
1296	
1297	**Rule 6**: REJECT the Question if it contains words like "commendable" and "innovatively" since these reviews are most likely generated by LLMs
1298	
1299	
1300	**Decision Logic Summary:**
1301	* A question MUST pass ALL applicable rules (1 -6) to be kept (`keep: true`).
1302	* Failure at any rule stage leads to rejection (`keep: false`).

You will be provided with a peer review containing a heading called "Questions" and another section called "Mixed Content". The "Questions" section contains multiple questions without any indication/ separator for a new question and the "Mixed Content" has a mix of questions that might not have a "?" to indicate a question. It can simply be a suggestion, an edit, a clarification required from the author etc.
1427	
1428	
1429	Task: Your Primary task is to Extract Questions first from the "Questions" section and then from the "Mixed Content" section. Perform verbatim extraction. I.e. Word-for-Word
1430	By Questions I mean all the questions --- explicitly or implicitly asked --- that the author needs to answer the reviewer based on the review text.
1431	
1432	
1433	1) Extract all the questions from the "Questions" section in a way all the sentences are retained. Do not miss any sentence or words from the original content in the section and output multiple Questions you have found, you need to break the Questions properly. If someone concatenates the multiple questions you have formed, they must get the "Questions" section as it is.
1434	2) While breaking the questions from the "Question" section, you might encounter nested questions. If both the parts are related keep them as a single question but if one part is an independent question, make them as separate questions.
1435	3) Extract all the questions that are present in the "Mixed Content" section. The questions might not be direct, it might include the reviewer telling what made him arrive at this question and then pose the question. It can also be some clarification he/she needs from a content in the paper. So include the complete context and don't simply output just the question.
1436	4) In some cases, the "Questions" section will direct you to refer the "Mixed Content" section by asking you to refer the weakness. That simply is your hint to find questions in the "Mixed Content" section.
1437	5) The "Mixed Content" section might have general observations or weaknesses of the paper, so only pick up questions,reviewer's suggestion for edits, reviewer seeking clarification BUT don't include general observations. This is the rule for "Mixed Content" section.
1438	
1439	
1440	Note: The "Questions" section will always have question present in it until unless it is blank or only asking you to refer to the weakness. The "Mixed Content" section might or might not have questions in it, so check very carefully. Learn from the zero-shot example below.
1441	Note 2: Important: When the questions that you form from "Questions" section are concatenated, it should form the original and complete content of the "Questions" section. This rule of concatenation is important and ONLY for "Questions" Section ONLY.
1442	
1443	Remember: Your task is just extraction of Questions and Not Rephrasing.
1444	
1445

results.content:
{
  "results": {
    "2509.19326": "This paper presents a systematic investigation into the capabilities and limitations of Large Language Models (LLMs) in the context of automated academic peer review. Below is a structured summary of the key contributions and their specific relevance to the generation of peer review questions.

### 1. Key Contributions of the Research

The work makes three primary contributions to the field of automated scientific evaluation (Section 1):

*   **High-Consensus Benchmark Dataset:** The authors constructed a dataset of **1,683 papers and 6,495 reviews** from ICLR (2024–2025) and NeurIPS (2023–2024). A unique feature of this dataset is the use of **Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)** to identify a consistency threshold for reviewer agreement, allowing the categorization of papers into "good," "borderline," and "weak" tiers while maintaining a balance between reliability and diversity (Section 3.1).
*   **Novel Evaluation Framework:** The study proposes a dual-perspective framework to assess reviews:
    *   **Semantic Similarity Analysis:** Uses the BGE-M3 embedding model to measure alignment between review components (Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions) and specific paper sections (IMRaD structure) (Section 3.3.1).
    *   **Knowledge Graph (KG) Methodology:** Uses the PL-Marker model to extract scientific entities and relations (following the SciERC schema). This enables the measurement of structural complexity (nodes, edges), conceptual diversity (label entropy), and contextual grounding (In-to-Out ratio) (Section 3.3.2).
*   **Comprehensive Behavioral Analysis:** The researchers conducted a comparative study of five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Qwen2.5-72B, and LLaMA3.3-70B), identifying a "leniency bias" and a lack of depth in critical analysis compared to human experts (Section 4).

---

### 2. Relation to Peer Review Question Generation

The paper’s findings offer specific insights into how LLMs perform when generating the **"Questions"** section of a peer review—a critical component intended to probe the authors on gaps or experimental details.

#### A. Evaluative Reasoning vs. Surface Description
The study finds that the "Questions" section of a review inherently behaves differently than the "Summary" or "Strengths" sections.
*   **Lower Semantic Similarity:** Analysis shows that "Questions" (along with "Weaknesses") have lower semantic similarity to the original paper text compared to "Summary" (Section 4.3). This is because questions require **evaluative reasoning and the introduction of external knowledge** rather than mere paraphrasing of the paper's content.
*   **In-to-Out Ratio:** For human reviewers, the "Questions" section has a high **In-to-Out Ratio** (e.g., 67.29% in ICLR 2025, Table 3). This indicates that humans frequently introduce concepts not explicitly found in the paper to formulate their questions. While LLMs follow this trend, they often remain more "surface-level" in their descriptions (Section 4.3).

#### B. Sensitivity to Paper Quality
The research highlights a significant "analytical gap" in how LLMs generate questions for papers of varying quality:
*   **Human Sensitivity:** Human reviewers exhibit a "bidirectional gradient." As paper quality decreases, humans generate more complex and diverse "Weaknesses" and "Questions." For example, in ICLR 2025, human-generated node counts in critical sections rose by **50.0%** for weak papers compared to good ones (Section 4.2).
*   **LLM Flattening:** In contrast, LLMs like GPT-4o exhibit "minimal structural variation." The complexity of the questions generated by LLMs remains relatively uniform regardless of whether the paper is high-quality or weak (Section 4.2, Figure 3). This suggests LLMs struggle to calibrate the intensity and depth of their questioning to the actual merits of the submission.

#### C. Structural Complexity of Generated Questions
The Knowledge Graph analysis (Table 3) reveals that some models (e.g., Gemini-2.0-Flash and Qwen-2.5-72B) actually **over-generate** entities in the "Questions" section, showing massive increases in node and edge counts compared to humans (e.g., Gemini-2.0-Flash showed a +116.65% increase in nodes for ICLR 2025 Questions). However, the paper concludes that this prolific generation does not equate to "analytical depth."     "2412.10415": "The research paper introduces **Generative Agent Reviewers (GAR)**, a framework designed to automate the peer review process using Large Language Model (LLM) agents. The system is engineered to provide high-quality feedback on research manuscripts and predict conference acceptance outcomes.

### Key Contributions

The paper identifies five primary contributions to the field of AI-assisted scientific review (Section 1):

1.  **The GAR Framework:** An end-to-end system that automates the transition from manuscript analysis to a final meta-review decision, providing detailed, on-demand feedback.
2.  **Contrastive Reviewer Personas:** A novel technique to extract granular reviewer profiles (strictness, focus areas, expertise) from single past reviews using **contrastive comparison** (Section 3.2.1).
3.  **Graph-Paper Representation:** A method for structuring academic documents into a knowledge graph that maps relationships between ideas, claims, technical details, and results. This reduces token consumption and captures logical links better than raw text (Section 3.1).
4.  **Multi-Round Review Process:** An iterative refinement workflow where agents generate an initial review and then improve it by retrieving similar claims and past reviewer comments from a **Memory Module** (Section 3.4).
5.  **Meta-Reviewer Agent:** An agent that synthesizes multiple individual reviews into a consistent final decision (Accept/Reject), emulating the role of an Area Chair (Section 3.5).

---

### Relation to Peer Review Question Generation

While the primary output of GAR is a structured review, its architecture and findings have significant implications for the task of **peer review question generation** (identifying specific, critical inquiries a reviewer should pose to authors).

#### 1. Targeted Questioning via Graph Decomposition
The **Graph-Paper Representation** (Section 3.1) decomposes a paper into specific nodes representing "ideas," "claims," and "technical details." By grouping these into "communities" using the Leiden algorithm, the system identifies distinct areas of a paper that require scrutiny. This allows for the generation of context-specific questions—for example, questioning the link between a specific "technical detail" node and its corresponding "result" node.

#### 2. Retrieval-Augmented Critique
The **Review Module** (Section 3.4) utilizes a retrieval-augmented scheme. When evaluating a specific idea in a paper, the agent retrieves the "Most Similar Claims" from a memory of past peer reviews.
*   **Example from Paper:** For a claim about GNN scalability, the system retrieves a similar claim from the memory module where a previous human reviewer asked: *"The dynamic graph approach is compelling but could benefit from further comparisons with static graph baselines."*
This mechanism directly facilitates the generation of relevant, grounded questions by looking at what human experts questioned in similar contexts previously.

#### 3. Persona-Driven Inquiry
Through the **Profile Module** (Section 3.2.1), GAR can generate questions from specific "personas." For instance:
*   A reviewer with high **Strictness** might generate questions regarding experimental flaws.
*   A reviewer with a **Technical Focus** might generate questions about mathematical proofs or implementation details.
The paper’s ablation study (Table 4) shows that these personas significantly impact the consistency and alignment of the review with human standards.

#### 4. Identifying Gaps in "Implications" and "Novelty"
The paper analyzes the frequency of feedback aspects (Figure 4). It notes that GAR is particularly effective at discussing the **"Implications of the Research"** (higher log frequency ratio than human reviewers). This capability is central to question generation, as it enables the agent to ask authors about the broader impact or long-term viability of their findings, a common requirement in high-tier peer review.

#### 5. Grounding Questions in Experimental Data
Unlike previous models that treat papers as plain text, GAR identifies and captions figures/charts using **Molmo-7b** (Section 4). This allows the system to generate questions based on specific empirical anomalies or results seen in graphs, such as asking for clarification on a specific bar chart or ablation study result.",
    "2502.11736": "This research paper introduces a framework for both generating and evaluating AI-based peer reviews for academic papers. Its contributions are centered on improving the reliability, depth, and adherence of large language model (LLM) outputs to scientific standards.

### Key Contributions

The paper outlines three primary research contributions:

1.  **ReviewEval Framework (§3):** A multi-dimensional evaluation framework designed to assess the quality of AI-generated reviews. Unlike previous metrics that focused primarily on similarity to human reviews, ReviewEval evaluates five distinct dimensions:
    *   **Comparison with Expert Reviews:** Uses **Semantic Similarity** (cosine similarity of embeddings, Eq. 1) and **Topic Coverage** (identifying conceptual overlap, Eq. 2).
    *   **Factual Correctness:** An automated pipeline to detect hallucinations and inaccuracies (§3.2).
    *   **Constructiveness:** Measures "Actionable Insights" based on clarity, feasibility, and implementation detail (Eq. 4).
    *   **Depth of Analysis:** Assesses critical engagement across five dimensions (e.g., soundness, contribution) on a 0–3 scale.
    *   **Adherence to Reviewer Guidelines:** Quantifies how well a review follows specific conference or journal criteria (§3.5).

2.  **ReviewAgent (§4):** An adaptive AI reviewer system that incorporates:
    *   **Conference-Specific Alignment:** Dynamically maps official guidelines to relevant sections of a paper using a prompt-mapping function (§4.1).
    *   **Iterative Refinement Loop:** A self-improvement mechanism that utilizes ReviewEval metrics to optimize intermediate outputs for better feedback quality.

3.  **Comprehensive Evaluation (§Results):** The authors benchmark ReviewAgent against state-of-the-art baselines (MARG and Sakana AI Scientist) using a diverse dataset of 120 papers (FullCorpus-120) across multiple domains and venues like ICLR, NeurIPS, and UAI (Tables 3 and 5).

---

### Relation to Peer Review Question Generation

The paper specifically integrates **question generation** as a core component of its "Factual Correctness" evaluation pipeline. This process is used to verify the validity of the AI's critique. The relationship is detailed in **Section 3.2 (Factual Correctness)**:

*   **Step 1: Question Generation:** For every AI-generated review ($R$), the system uses an LLM to convert the review's claims into a **structured verification question ($Q$)**. This question is designed to "capture its central critique."
*   **Step 2: Query Decomposition:** The generated question $Q$ is then decomposed into a set of fine-grained **sub-questions** ($q_1, \dots, q_n$). These sub-questions isolate distinct aspects of the reviewer's claim.
*   **Step 3: Verification via Answer Generation:** These questions are fed into a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system. The system generates answers ($A_i$) by retrieving context directly from the source paper.
*   **Purpose:** This "question generation" mechanism serves as the foundation for an **automated rebuttal process**. If the answers derived from the paper (via the generated questions) do not support the claims made in the AI review, the review is flagged for factual inaccuracy or hallucination.

In summary, the paper treats question generation not as an end-goal for a human reviewer, but as a **diagnostic tool** to ensure that an AI reviewer’s critiques are grounded in the actual evidence presented in the manuscript (Figure 2, Column 2).",
    "2506.07642": "Based on the research paper provided, here is a structured summary of the key contributions and their specific relationship to peer review question generation.

### **Overview of TreeReview**
The paper introduces **TreeReview**, a hierarchical, question-driven framework designed to assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in providing in-depth feedback on lengthy scientific manuscripts. It addresses three primary limitations in current LLM-driven review methods: capturing long-range dependencies, overcoming superficial analysis, and reducing the high computational costs associated with complex multi-agent frameworks (Section 1).

---

### **Key Contributions**

1.  **The TreeReview Framework:** A novel two-stage architecture that structures the peer review process as a hierarchical reasoning task (Section 3.1).
    *   **Top-Down Stage:** Recursively decomposes a high-level review task into increasingly fine-grained sub-questions (Section 3.2).
    *   **Bottom-Up Stage:** Answers leaf questions using relevant paper chunks and aggregates these answers to synthesize a comprehensive final review (Section 3.3).
2.  **Open-Source Evaluation Benchmark:** The authors constructed a benchmark using papers and human reviews from **ICLR 2024** and **NeurIPS 2023**. This dataset facilitates evaluation for two tasks: full review generation and actionable feedback comment generation (Section 4.1).
3.  **Superior Performance and Efficiency:** Experimental results show that *TreeReview* outperforms strong baselines (such as MARG and SEA-E) in LLM-as-judge scores for specificity, technical depth, and comprehensiveness (Fig. 2). Notably, it achieves higher alignment with human feedback while reducing token usage by **80.2%** compared to the MARG multi-agent framework (Table 6).
4.  **Domain Generalization:** The authors demonstrated the framework's robustness by applying it to non-AI fields, including biology, climate science, and quantum science, using papers from *Nature Communications* (Section 4.5).

---

### **Relating Contributions to Peer Review Question Generation**

The paper positions **question generation** as the central mechanism for high-quality review. The contributions relate to this task through three specific strategies:

#### **1. Top-Down Hierarchical Decomposition**
*TreeReview* uses a **Question Generator agent ($M_q$)** to create a review question tree ($T$).
*   **Source Material:** To ensure exploratory and broad questions, the generator uses only paper **metadata** (title, abstract, table of contents) rather than the full text (Section 3.2).
*   **Depth-Awareness:** The framework follows a specific logic: root-level questions cover major aspects (novelty, methodology), while deeper levels (up to a maximum depth of $D=4$) probe specific technical details (Section 4.1).
*   **MECE Principle:** Generated sub-questions must be **Mutually Exclusive and Collectively Exhaustive**, ensuring zero overlap while covering the entire scope of the parent question (Section 3.2).

#### **2. Dynamic Question Expansion**
A critical contribution to question generation is the **dynamic expansion mechanism**. 
*   **Function:** During the bottom-up stage, the **Answer Synthesizer ($M_a$)** evaluates if current answers are sufficient to resolve an intermediate question. If not, it generates up to $W_{exp}=2$ follow-up questions to probe unaddressed or ambiguous aspects (Section 3.3).
*   **Impact:** This mechanism was triggered for **38.54%** of non-leaf questions, generating an average of **25.6 additional questions per review**. Case studies showed these dynamically expanded questions were often responsible for identifying the most critical and specific paper issues (Section 4.6).

#### **3. Question-Aware Context Retrieval**
The framework links question generation to efficient information retrieval. 
*   **Equation 2:** Leaf questions are answered by identifying the top-$k$ (specifically $k=3$) most relevant paper chunks (Section 3.3). 
*   **Mechanism:** It uses a question-aware context compression method (LongLLMLingua) to calculate the probability of a question given a specific chunk. This ensures the LLM's answers—and subsequent reviews—are grounded in concrete evidence rather than general summaries (Section 3.3).

### **Conclusion**
In summary, *TreeReview* contributes a method where peer review is not treated as a single generation task, but as a **structured inquiry process**. By generating a dynamic tree of questions, the framework forces the LLM to perform "local" analysis of technical details while maintaining "global" assessment through systematic aggregation.",
    "2510.12367": "This summary outlines the key contributions of the paper "LLM-REVal: LLM Reviewer Re-Evaluation through a Multi-round Simulation of the Academic Publication Process" and explains their relevance to the context of peer review question generation and feedback.

### **Structured Summary of Key Contributions**

The paper introduces **LLM-REVal**, a multi-round simulation framework designed to evaluate the systemic risks and biases of using Large Language Models (LLMs) as peer reviewers.

#### **1. Development of a Closed-Loop Research-Review Simulation**
The authors constructed a two-agent system to emulate the scholarly lifecycle (Section 3):
*   **Research Agent:** Performs literature retrieval (via Semantic Scholar/Google Search), idea generation, experimental design, and manuscript compilation using LaTeX templates (Section 3.1).
*   **Review Agent:** A five-stage pipeline simulating the ICLR workflow: Initial Assessment, Author-Reviewer Discussion (rebuttals), Reassessment, Meta-Review, and Final Decision (Section 3.2).

#### **2. Identification of Salient LLM Reviewer Patterns**
Through six rounds of simulation involving 100 human-authored and multiple LLM-authored papers, the study identified three distinct patterns (Section 5):
*   **LLM-Authored Superiority:** LLM reviewers assigned significantly higher scores to LLM-generated papers (78% acceptance rate) than to human papers on the same topics (49% acceptance rate) (Table 1, Figure 2).
*   **Revision Boost:** Both human and LLM-authored papers showed statistically significant score improvements (average increases of +0.36 and +0.30, respectively) when revised according to LLM-generated feedback (Section 5.2).
*   **Inevitable Rejection:** Despite multiple revision cycles, a subset of human-authored papers remained persistently low-scored, even when human experts deemed the work valuable (Section 5.2, Section 6).

#### **3. Discovery of Systematic Biases**
The paper identifies the underlying drivers of these scoring patterns (Section 7):
*   **Linguistic Feature Bias:** LLM reviewers prefer text that is concise, lexically diverse (higher proportion of distinct n-grams), and lexically complex (higher Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level) (Section 7.1, Figure 4).
*   **Critical Statement Aversion:** Human papers addressing "negative" topics (e.g., risks, fairness, limitations) were systematically undervalued. In human papers, the frequency of negative keywords correlated with lower scores, whereas in LLM papers, scores increased even with negative keywords due to "positive framing" (Section 7.2, Figure 6).

---

### **Relationship to Peer Review Question Generation**

While the paper focuses on the evaluation of LLM reviewers, its findings have direct implications for the generation of review questions and feedback during the peer-review process:

#### **Effectiveness of LLM-Generated Feedback/Questions**
The study provides empirical evidence that LLM-generated critiques can be actionable. In the **"Author-Reviewer Discussion"** stage (Section 3.2), the system generates critiques that authors must address. The **"Human check for Revision Boost"** (Section 6) found that **46.55% of issues** identified by the LLM reviewer were successfully resolved through textual edits, leading to higher subsequent scores. This suggests that LLMs are capable of generating constructive questions that can improve manuscript quality.

#### **Risks in Question Framing and Content**
The research highlights a significant risk in how LLMs generate and react to "critical" questions:
*   **Framing Bias:** Section 7.2 reveals that LLMs struggle to remain impartial when a paper uses "negative framing" to discuss risks or limitations. If an LLM-generated review focuses its questions on these negative aspects, it may lead to an unfairly low score, whereas an LLM-authored paper discussing the same risks with a positive tone receives higher scores.
*   **Superficial Critique:** The "Linguistic Feature" analysis (Section 7.1) suggests that LLM reviewers may generate feedback that prioritizes stylistic markers (conciseness and lexical complexity) over substantive scientific depth. This implies that the "questions" generated by an LLM might inadvertently push human authors to adopt an "LLM-style" of writing rather than focusing on methodological improvements.

#### **Early-Stage Support vs. Fairness**
The authors conclude that while the LLM-as-reviewer paradigm is effective for **supporting early-stage researchers** by providing iterative feedback (Takeaway 4, Section 7), it remains an unreliable tool for final evaluative judgments due to its susceptibility to stylistic polishing (Section 7.1). The finding that **"LLM polishing"** alone can raise a paper's score enough to move it from rejection to acceptance (Section 7.1) suggests that LLM-generated review questions may be biased toward the presentation of the text rather than its underlying scientific merit."

    "2509.19326": "This paper presents a systematic investigation into the capabilities and limitations of Large Language Models (LLMs) in the context of automated academic peer review. Below is a structured summary of the key contributions and their specific relevance to the generation of peer review questions.

### 1. Key Contributions of the Research

The work makes three primary contributions to the field of automated scientific evaluation (Section 1):

*   **High-Consensus Benchmark Dataset:** The authors constructed a dataset of **1,683 papers and 6,495 reviews** from ICLR (2024–2025) and NeurIPS (2023–2024). A unique feature of this dataset is the use of **Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)** to identify a consistency threshold for reviewer agreement, allowing the categorization of papers into "good," "borderline," and "weak" tiers while maintaining a balance between reliability and diversity (Section 3.1).
*   **Novel Evaluation Framework:** The study proposes a dual-perspective framework to assess reviews:
    *   **Semantic Similarity Analysis:** Uses the BGE-M3 embedding model to measure alignment between review components (Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions) and specific paper sections (IMRaD structure) (Section 3.3.1).
    *   **Knowledge Graph (KG) Methodology:** Uses the PL-Marker model to extract scientific entities and relations (following the SciERC schema). This enables the measurement of structural complexity (nodes, edges), conceptual diversity (label entropy), and contextual grounding (In-to-Out ratio) (Section 3.3.2).
*   **Comprehensive Behavioral Analysis:** The researchers conducted a comparative study of five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Qwen2.5-72B, and LLaMA3.3-70B), identifying a "leniency bias" and a lack of depth in critical analysis compared to human experts (Section 4).

---

### 2. Relation to Peer Review Question Generation

The paper’s findings offer specific insights into how LLMs perform when generating the **"Questions"** section of a peer review—a critical component intended to probe the authors on gaps or experimental details.

#### A. Evaluative Reasoning vs. Surface Description
The study finds that the "Questions" section of a review inherently behaves differently than the "Summary" or "Strengths" sections.
*   **Lower Semantic Similarity:** Analysis shows that "Questions" (along with "Weaknesses") have lower semantic similarity to the original paper text compared to "Summary" (Section 4.3). This is because questions require **evaluative reasoning and the introduction of external knowledge** rather than mere paraphrasing of the paper's content.
*   **In-to-Out Ratio:** For human reviewers, the "Questions" section has a high **In-to-Out Ratio** (e.g., 67.29% in ICLR 2025, Table 3). This indicates that humans frequently introduce concepts not explicitly found in the paper to formulate their questions. While LLMs follow this trend, they often remain more "surface-level" in their descriptions (Section 4.3).

#### B. Sensitivity to Paper Quality
The research highlights a significant "analytical gap" in how LLMs generate questions for papers of varying quality:
*   **Human Sensitivity:** Human reviewers exhibit a "bidirectional gradient." As paper quality decreases, humans generate more complex and diverse "Weaknesses" and "Questions." For example, in ICLR 2025, human-generated node counts in critical sections rose by **50.0%** for weak papers compared to good ones (Section 4.2).
*   **LLM Flattening:** In contrast, LLMs like GPT-4o exhibit "minimal structural variation." The complexity of the questions generated by LLMs remains relatively uniform regardless of whether the paper is high-quality or weak (Section 4.2, Figure 3). This suggests LLMs struggle to calibrate the intensity and depth of their questioning to the actual merits of the submission.

#### C. Structural Complexity of Generated Questions
The Knowledge Graph analysis (Table 3) reveals that some models (e.g., Gemini-2.0-Flash and Qwen-2.5-72B) actually **over-generate** entities in the "Questions" section, showing massive increases in node and edge counts compared to humans (e.g., Gemini-2.0-Flash showed a +116.65% increase in nodes for ICLR 2025 Questions). However, the paper concludes that this prolific generation does not equate to "analytical depth." While LLMs are "more prolific in generating content in affirmational sections," they fall short in the "nuanced evaluative reasoning" required to detect subtle flaws and ask substantive, critical questions (Section 4.4).

### Summary Table: "Questions" Section Metrics (ICLR 2025)

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Num Nodes** | 5.79–6.13 | -12.34% to -16.66% | +21.80% to +33.28% |
| **Label Entropy** | 1.00–1.08 | +30.18% to +37.18% | +51.76% to +62.02% |
| **In-to-Out Ratio** | 63.45%–67.29% | 55.61%–60.66% | 38.63%–41.40% |

*Source: Grounded in Table 3 and Section 4.*",

*   **Impact:** This mechanism was triggered for **38.54%** of non-leaf questions, generating an average of **25.6 additional questions per review**. Case studies showed these dynamically expanded questions were often responsible for identifying the most critical and specific paper issues (Section 4.6).

#### **3. Question-Aware Context Retrieval**
The framework links question generation to efficient information retrieval. 
*   **Equation 2:** Leaf questions are answered by identifying the top-$k$ (specifically $k=3$) most relevant paper chunks (Section 3.3). 
*   **Mechanism:** It uses a question-aware context compression method (LongLLMLingua) to calculate the probability of a question given a specific chunk. This ensures the LLM's answers—and subsequent reviews—are grounded in concrete evidence rather than general summaries (Section 3.3).

### **Conclusion**
In summary, *TreeReview* contributes a method where peer review is not treated as a single generation task, but as a **structured inquiry process**. By generating a dynamic tree of questions, the framework forces the LLM to perform "local" analysis of technical details while maintaining "global" assessment through systematic aggregation.",
    "2510.12367": "This summary outlines the key contributions of the paper "LLM-REVal: LLM Reviewer Re-Evaluation through a Multi-round Simulation of the Academic Publication Process" and explains their relevance to the context of peer review question generation and feedback.

### **Structured Summary of Key Contributions**

The paper introduces **LLM-REVal**, a multi-round simulation framework designed to evaluate the systemic risks and biases of using Large Language Models (LLMs) as peer reviewers.

#### **1. Development of a Closed-Loop Research-Review Simulation**
The authors constructed a two-agent system to emulate the scholarly lifecycle (Section 3):
*   **Research Agent:** Performs literature retrieval (via Semantic Scholar/Google Search), idea generation, experimental design, and manuscript compilation using LaTeX templates (Section 3.1).
*   **Review Agent:** A five-stage pipeline simulating the ICLR workflow: Initial Assessment, Author-Reviewer Discussion (rebuttals), Reassessment, Meta-Review, and Final Decision (Section 3.2).

#### **2. Identification of Salient LLM Reviewer Patterns**
Through six rounds of simulation involving 100 human-authored and multiple LLM-authored papers, the study identified three distinct patterns (Section 5):
*   **LLM-Authored Superiority:** LLM reviewers assigned significantly higher scores to LLM-generated papers (78% acceptance rate) than to human papers on the same topics (49% acceptance rate) (Table 1, Figure 2).
*   **Revision Boost:** Both human and LLM-authored papers showed statistically significant score improvements (average increases of +0.36 and +0.30, respectively) when revised according to LLM-generated feedback (Section 5.2).
*   **Inevitable Rejection:** Despite multiple revision cycles, a subset of human-authored papers remained persistently low-scored, even when human experts deemed the work valuable (Section 5.2, Section 6).

#### **3. Discovery of Systematic Biases**
The paper identifies the underlying drivers of these scoring patterns (Section 7):
*   **Linguistic Feature Bias:** LLM reviewers prefer text that is concise, lexically diverse (higher proportion of distinct n-grams), and lexically complex (higher Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level) (Section 7.1, Figure 4).
*   **Critical Statement Aversion:** Human papers addressing "negative" topics (e.g., risks, fairness, limitations) were systematically undervalued. In human papers, the frequency of negative keywords correlated with lower scores, whereas in LLM papers, scores increased even with negative keywords due to "positive framing" (Section 7.2, Figure 6).

---

### **Relationship to Peer Review Question Generation**

While the paper focuses on the evaluation of LLM reviewers, its findings have direct implications for the generation of review questions and feedback during the peer-review process:

#### **Effectiveness of LLM-Generated Feedback/Questions**
The study provides empirical evidence that LLM-generated critiques can be actionable. In the **"Author-Reviewer Discussion"** stage (Section 3.2), the system generates critiques that authors must address. The **"Human check for Revision Boost"** (Section 6) found that **46.55% of issues** identified by the LLM reviewer were successfully resolved through textual edits, leading to higher subsequent scores. This suggests that LLMs are capable of generating constructive questions that can improve manuscript quality.

#### **Risks in Question Framing and Content**
The research highlights a significant risk in how LLMs generate and react to "critical" questions:
*   **Framing Bias:** Section 7.2 reveals that LLMs struggle to remain impartial when a paper uses "negative framing" to discuss risks or limitations. If an LLM-generated review focuses its questions on these negative aspects, it may lead to an unfairly low score, whereas an LLM-authored paper discussing the same risks with a positive tone receives higher scores.
*   **Superficial Critique:** The "Linguistic Feature" analysis (Section 7.1) suggests that LLM reviewers may generate feedback that prioritizes stylistic markers (conciseness and lexical complexity) over substantive scientific depth. This implies that the "questions" generated by an LLM might inadvertently push human authors to adopt an "LLM-style" of writing rather than focusing on methodological improvements.

#### **Early-Stage Support vs. Fairness**
The authors conclude that while the LLM-as-reviewer paradigm is effective for **supporting early-stage researchers** by providing iterative feedback (Takeaway 4, Section 7), it remains an unreliable tool for final evaluative judgments due to its susceptibility to stylistic polishing (Section 7.1). The finding that **"LLM polishing"** alone can raise a paper's score enough to move it from rejection to acceptance (Section 7.1) suggests that LLM-generated review questions may be biased toward the presentation of the text rather than its underlying scientific merit."
  }
}
